content:
# Leviticus Chapter 19 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 69r–69v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 105–106
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_019.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 19 is the ethical and theological apex of the Holiness Code (Lev 17–26) and, by the consensus of ancient and modern readers alike, one of the most compositionally remarkable chapters in the entire Torah. Its governing formula — "You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy" (v. 2) — is the fountainhead of a social ethic that moves across cultic, agricultural, commercial, legal, and interpersonal domains without division. The chapter is unified structurally by the refrain "I am the LORD" / "I am the LORD your God," which appears eighteen times and grounds each successive law in the divine identity rather than in abstract moral reasoning.

The chapter climaxes twice: at v. 18b, ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha ("you shall love your neighbor as yourself"), which Rabbi Akiva called "the greatest principle in the Torah" and which Jesus cited as the second commandment equal to the love of God (Matt 22:37–40; Mark 12:28–34); and at v. 34, where the identical command is extended to the sojourner — ve-ahavta lo kamokha ("you shall love him as yourself") — grounded in Israel's own historical memory of vulnerability: "for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt."

**Key term rulings for this chapter:**
- YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel"
- ger = "sojourner" throughout (NOT "foreigner" or "stranger" — see ruling at vv. 10, 33–34)
- pe'ah = "corner" (with bracketed gloss where first introduced)
- leqet = "gleanings"
- shelamim = "peace offerings"
- pigul = "offensive thing" (v. 7)
- hokhei'ach tokhiach = "you shall surely rebuke" (EMET's absolute-infinitive rendering preferred)
- mozne/avne/eifat/hin tsedeq = "balances/weights/ephah/hin of righteousness" (EMET preferred over LOGOS's "just" for tsedeq consistency with vv. 15, 35–36)
- hillulim = "offering of praise" (LOGOS/PNEUMA preferred; more natural than EMET's "praise-offering")
- ovot = "mediums" | yid'onim = "spirits of the dead" (EMET preferred over LOGOS's "necromancers" and PNEUMA's "spiritists")
- English name forms throughout (Moses, not Moshe)
- mishpatai = "my ordinances" (v. 37; following PNEUMA/project precedent over LOGOS's "rules")

---

## Consensus Translation

**19:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**19:2** "Speak to the entire congregation of the sons of Israel and say to them: You shall be holy, for I — the LORD your God — am holy.

**19:3** Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father, and you shall keep my sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.

**19:4** Do not turn to the idols, and you shall not make for yourselves molten gods. I am the LORD your God.

**19:5** And when you offer a sacrifice of peace offerings to the LORD, you shall offer it for your acceptance.

**19:6** On the day of your sacrifice it shall be eaten, and on the next day; and what remains until the third day shall be burned with fire.

**19:7** And if it is eaten at all on the third day — it is an offensive thing [pigul]; it will not be accepted.

**19:8** And everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity, because he has profaned what is holy to the LORD; and that person shall be cut off from among his people.

**19:9** And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap to the corner [pe'ah] of your field, and the gleanings [leqet] of your harvest you shall not gather.

**19:10** And your vineyard you shall not strip bare, and the fallen grapes of your vineyard you shall not gather; for the poor and for the sojourner you shall leave them. I am the LORD your God.

**19:11** You shall not steal, and you shall not deal falsely, and you shall not lie to one another.

**19:12** And you shall not swear by my name falsely, and so profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.

**19:13** You shall not oppress your neighbor and you shall not rob him; the wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you overnight until morning.

**19:14** You shall not curse the deaf, and before the blind you shall not put a stumbling block; and you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**19:15** You shall do no injustice in judgment; you shall not be partial to the poor and you shall not defer to the great; in righteousness you shall judge your neighbor.

**19:16** You shall not go about as a slanderer among your people; you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor. I am the LORD.

**19:17** You shall not hate your brother in your heart; you shall surely rebuke your neighbor and you shall not bear sin because of him.

**19:18** You shall not take vengeance and you shall not bear a grudge against the sons of your people; and you shall love your neighbor as yourself [ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha]. I am the LORD.

**19:19** My statutes you shall keep: your cattle you shall not cause to breed with a different kind; your field you shall not sow with two kinds of seed; and a garment of two kinds of mixed material shall not come upon you.

**19:20** And a man who lies with a woman — a slave-woman pledged to a man, and she has not been redeemed nor given her freedom — an inquiry shall be held; they shall not be put to death, because she was not free.

**19:21** And he shall bring his guilt offering to the LORD, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — a ram as a guilt offering.

**19:22** And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering before the LORD for his sin that he sinned; and he shall be forgiven for his sin that he sinned.

**19:23** And when you come into the land and plant any tree for food, you shall treat its fruit as forbidden [va-araltem orlato — its foreskin, its uncircumcised fruit]: three years it shall be forbidden to you; it shall not be eaten.

**19:24** And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy, an offering of praise to the LORD.

**19:25** And in the fifth year you may eat its fruit, to increase its yield for you. I am the LORD your God.

**19:26** You shall not eat upon the blood. You shall not practice divination and you shall not practice augury.

**19:27** You shall not round off the corner [pe'ah] of your head, and you shall not mar the corner [pe'ah] of your beard.

**19:28** And a cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh] you shall not make in your flesh, and tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa'] you shall not put on yourselves. I am the LORD.

**19:29** Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute, lest the land fall to prostitution and the land become full of depravity [zimmah].

**19:30** My sabbaths you shall keep, and my sanctuary you shall revere. I am the LORD.

**19:31** Do not turn to the mediums [ha-ovot] and to the spirits of the dead [yid'onim] — do not seek them out to be defiled by them. I am the LORD your God.

**19:32** Before a gray head you shall rise, and you shall honor the face of an elder, and you shall fear your God. I am the LORD.

**19:33** And when a sojourner sojourns with you in your land, you shall not wrong him.

**19:34** As a native among you shall the sojourner who sojourns with you be to you, and you shall love him as yourself [ve-ahavta lo kamokha], for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

**19:35** You shall do no injustice in judgment, in measure, in weight, or in quantity.

**19:36** Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq], weights of righteousness [avne tsedeq], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq] — shall be to you. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.

**19:37** And you shall keep all my statutes and all my ordinances, and you shall do them. I am the LORD."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula / name form.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — project convention uses English name forms throughout (established from Genesis onward).

**V. 2 — "entire congregation" vs. "whole community."** EMET/LOGOS: "entire congregation of the sons of Israel." PNEUMA: "whole community of Israel." Consensus: "entire congregation of the sons of Israel" — kol adat bney Yisra'el is the precise legal term for the assembled covenant community; "congregation" for eda is the established Leviticus-wide rendering. PNEUMA's "whole community" loses the formal legal register of eda and drops the "sons of Israel" phrase that the Hebrew carries. The unusual address to the entire congregation (not just priests or leaders) is the theological point of the verse; preserving the legal vocabulary makes that point visible.

**V. 2 — "for I — the LORD your God — am holy."** EMET: "for I — the LORD your God — am holy." LOGOS: "for I the LORD your God am holy." PNEUMA: "because I — the LORD your God — am holy." Consensus: EMET's form, using dashes to set off the divine self-identification in apposition. The Hebrew places qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem with YHWH in emphatic apposition; the dashes preserve the rhetorical pause. PNEUMA's "because" for ki is acceptable but slightly weakens the grounding force; "for" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred.

**V. 3 — "revere" vs. "honor."** EMET/LOGOS: "revere." PNEUMA: "honor." Consensus: "revere" — the Hebrew tira'u (from yare, "to fear, to stand in awe of") is the stronger term and is deliberately distinct from kaved ("honor," the Decalogue's word at Exod 20:12). The choice of yare here is significant: the text uses the stronger term. "Revere" preserves that distinction; "honor" flattens it into the Decalogue word.

**V. 3 — "Each man" vs. "Every one of you."** EMET: "Each man his mother and his father." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father." Consensus: "Every one of you" — the Hebrew ish immo ve-aviv tira'u uses ish ("each man/person") as the distributive subject, but the verb is plural (tira'u), addressed to the whole congregation. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "Every one of you" captures the plural-distributive sense more naturally in English. EMET's hyper-literal object-first word order ("Each man his mother and his father") reproduces the Hebrew syntax but is awkward in English running prose.

**V. 3 — "sabbaths" vs. "Sabbaths."** EMET: lowercase "sabbaths." LOGOS: capitalized "Sabbaths." PNEUMA: lowercase "sabbaths." Consensus: lowercase "sabbaths" — consistent with project convention for lower-casing institutional/calendrical terms unless a proper name.

**V. 4 — "idols" and "molten gods."** EMET: "Do not turn to the idols [ha-elilim], and molten gods [elohei massekah] you shall not make for yourselves." LOGOS: "Do not turn to idols or make for yourselves cast images of gods." PNEUMA: "Do not turn to idols or make gods of cast metal for yourselves." Consensus: "Do not turn to the idols, and you shall not make for yourselves molten gods" — retaining the definite article "the idols" (ha-elilim, MT has the article), which LOGOS/PNEUMA drop; EMET's "molten gods" is preferred over LOGOS's "cast images of gods" (which separates "gods" from the cast-image concept) and PNEUMA's "gods of cast metal." "Molten gods" (elohei massekah) is a single Hebrew phrase best rendered as a compound noun. Hebrew-transliteration glosses are omitted in the consensus text body (per format).

**V. 5 — "offer/slaughter" and "for your acceptance."** EMET: "slaughter a sacrifice of peace-offerings... slaughter it for your acceptance." LOGOS: "offer a sacrifice of peace offerings... offer it so that you may be accepted." PNEUMA: "offer a peace offering... offer it in a way that you can be accepted." Consensus: "offer a sacrifice of peace offerings... offer it for your acceptance" — EMET's "slaughter" for the Hiphil of zevach is defensible (the root means to slaughter/sacrifice) but "offer" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the more natural English term for the act as a whole; the word "sacrifice" in the phrase "sacrifice of peace offerings" preserves the slaughter-connotation implicitly. "For your acceptance" (liretzonkhem) is the most compact and precise of the three renderings; it is the technical sacrificial term for a valid offering and EMET's formula is preferred over LOGOS/PNEUMA's paraphrases.

**V. 6 — "day of your sacrifice/slaughtering/offering."** EMET: "day of your slaughtering." LOGOS: "day of your sacrifice." PNEUMA: "day you offer it." Consensus: "day of your sacrifice" — LOGOS strikes the best balance between precision and readability. EMET's "slaughtering" is technically accurate but rhythmically awkward in running prose; PNEUMA's "day you offer it" loses the nominal form.

**V. 6 — "on the next day" vs. "the morrow."** EMET: "on the morrow." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "on the next day." Consensus: "on the next day" — more natural modern English; "the morrow" is an archaism that adds stylistic inconsistency without exegetical benefit.

**V. 6 — "burned with fire" vs. variants.** EMET: "in fire it shall be burned." LOGOS: "burned with fire." PNEUMA: "burned up." Consensus: "burned with fire" (LOGOS) — EMET's "in fire" is a slightly unusual English preposition; "burned up" (PNEUMA) adds a completive sense not required by the Hebrew ba-esh yisaref.

**V. 7 — pigul rendering.** EMET: "a putrid thing [pigul]." LOGOS: "an offensive thing." PNEUMA: "tainted." Consensus: "an offensive thing [pigul]" — combining LOGOS's rendering with EMET's bracketed preservation of the Hebrew technical term. PNEUMA's "tainted" is too mild and loses the ritual-invalidity dimension of the term. EMET's "putrid" overemphasizes the physical-decay sense; pigul connotes sacral offensiveness more than literal putridity. "Offensive thing" (LOGOS, following ESV) is the best formal equivalent.

**V. 8 — "the one who eats" vs. "everyone who eats."** EMET: "the one who eats it shall bear his iniquity." LOGOS: "everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity." PNEUMA: "Anyone who eats it will bear the guilt of that offense." Consensus: "everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity" — LOGOS's "everyone" better captures the distributive force of the Hebrew ha-okhlo (the participle with article functioning as a distributive); PNEUMA's "guilt of that offense" and "will bear" introduce paraphrase and a shift to future tense not in the Hebrew.

**V. 8 — "profaned what is holy to the LORD" vs. variants.** EMET: "the holy thing of the LORD he has profaned." LOGOS: "he has profaned what is holy to the LORD." PNEUMA: "they have profaned what is holy to the LORD." Consensus: LOGOS — "he has profaned what is holy to the LORD." EMET's word order ("the holy thing... he has profaned") mirrors Hebrew but is awkward; PNEUMA's "they" shifts to plural inconsistently with the singular subject of the verse.

**V. 9 — pe'ah and leqet rendering.** EMET: "not complete the corner [pe'at] of your field... the gleaning [leqet] of your harvest." LOGOS: "not reap your field right to its edge... the gleanings of your harvest." PNEUMA: "do not harvest all the way to the edge of your field... do not go back to pick up what the harvesters missed." Consensus: "not reap to the corner [pe'ah] of your field, and the gleanings [leqet] of your harvest you shall not gather" — preserves the technical terms pe'ah and leqet with brackets on first occurrence (v. 9), since these are project-designated preserve terms. LOGOS's "right to its edge" is interpretive paraphrase of pe'ah; PNEUMA's description of the leqet ("do not go back to pick up what the harvesters missed") is a dynamic expansion that loses the technical term entirely. EMET's "complete the corner" follows MT syntax but "reap to the corner" is more idiomatic English while preserving pe'ah's sense.

**V. 10 — "strip bare" vs. "go over a second time."** EMET: "you shall not go over a second time [lo te'olel]." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "you shall not strip your vineyard bare." Consensus: "you shall not strip your vineyard bare" — LOGOS/PNEUMA's rendering is more natural English for lo te'olel (Polel of alal, "to glean completely"). EMET's "go over a second time" is accurate to the root's sense of gleaning-over but is less transparent to the English reader.

**V. 10 — "sojourner" vs. "foreigner."** EMET/LOGOS: "sojourner." PNEUMA: "foreigner living among you." Consensus: "sojourner" — this is a binding terminology ruling. The Hebrew ger is the project's established term requiring "sojourner" (see chapter brief and project memory). PNEUMA's "foreigner" violates this ruling and is not adopted. The ger is not simply a foreigner but a resident alien with ongoing legal and social ties to the community; "foreigner" loses this precision.

**V. 11 — "deal falsely" vs. "deny falsely" vs. "deceive."** EMET: "you shall not deny falsely [lo tekachashу]." LOGOS: "you shall not deal falsely." PNEUMA: "Do not deceive one another." Consensus: "you shall not deal falsely" (LOGOS) — the Hebrew tekachashу (Pi'el of kachash) means to act deceptively, to lie in conduct; "deal falsely" (LOGOS) captures the behavioral dimension. EMET's "deny falsely" is a legitimate rendering but "deny" in modern English implies a specific speech-act of denial rather than the broader deceptive conduct kachash describes. PNEUMA's "deceive one another" is acceptable but merges the distinction from lo teshaqru ("you shall not lie") in the same verse. The three prohibitions (steal / deal falsely / lie) should maintain distinct wordings.

**V. 11 — "one to his neighbor" vs. "to one another."** EMET: "one to his neighbor." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "to one another." Consensus: "to one another" — the Hebrew ish le-amito ("each to his fellow") is best rendered idiomatically as "to one another" in English; EMET's "one to his neighbor" is literal but slightly stilted.

**V. 12 — "and so profane" vs. "and you shall profane."** EMET: "you shall profane the name of your God." LOGOS: "and so profane the name of your God." PNEUMA: "that would be profaning your God's name." Consensus: "and so profane the name of your God" (LOGOS) — the waw-consecutive in the Hebrew (ve-chillalta) indicates consequence: swearing falsely results in profaning the Name. LOGOS's "and so" captures that consequential force. EMET's simple "and you shall profane" reads as a second independent command; PNEUMA's "that would be profaning" is a paraphrase that loses the verbal force.

**V. 13 — "hired man" vs. "hired worker."** EMET: "hired man [sekhar sakir]." LOGOS: "hired worker." PNEUMA: "hired worker." Consensus: "hired worker" — sakir is a laborer for hire (male or female); "worker" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is more inclusive than "man" and is the better modern equivalent. The phrase "wages of a hired worker" (LOGOS) is cleaner than EMET's "wages of a hired man" or PNEUMA's inversion "a hired worker's wages."

**V. 14 — "in front of" addition.** EMET/LOGOS: "before the blind you shall not put a stumbling block." PNEUMA: "do not put a stumbling block in front of the blind." Consensus: "before the blind you shall not put a stumbling block" — "before" is the literal Hebrew lifnei; PNEUMA's "in front of" is a synonym but the consensus preserves the more established rendering.

**V. 14 — "you shall fear your God" vs. "Fear your God."** EMET/LOGOS: "you shall fear your God." PNEUMA: "Fear your God." Consensus: "you shall fear your God" — the Hebrew ve-yare'ta is an imperfect used as a command (not a qal imperative); the "you shall" form (EMET/LOGOS) better represents the second-person imperfect's modal force and maintains stylistic consistency with the chapter's predominant command-form.

**V. 15 — judicial partiality language.** EMET: "you shall not show partiality to the poor... you shall not honor the great." LOGOS: "you shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great." PNEUMA: "Do not show favoritism to the poor or deference to the powerful." Consensus: "you shall not be partial to the poor and you shall not defer to the great" — LOGOS's verbs ("be partial," "defer") are the most precise. EMET's "show partiality" and "honor the great" (lo tahdar pene gadol) are acceptable; "honor" for hadar slightly misses the sycophantic-deference nuance. PNEUMA's "powerful" for gadol ("great") is an interpretation; gadol means "great/important" which may be power-based but is not identical to "powerful."

**V. 15 — "your neighbor" vs. "your neighbor with fairness."** EMET/LOGOS: "judge your neighbor." PNEUMA: "judge your neighbor with fairness." Consensus: "judge your neighbor" — be-tsedeq tishpot amitekha is "in righteousness you shall judge your neighbor"; the "in righteousness" phrase precedes the verb in the Hebrew. Consensus word order: "in righteousness you shall judge your neighbor" — preserving the Hebrew emphasis on righteousness as the qualifying adverb. PNEUMA's "with fairness" appended after "neighbor" shifts the emphasis.

**V. 16 — "stand against the blood" vs. "stand by while... life is at stake."** EMET/LOGOS: "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor." PNEUMA: "Do not stand by while your neighbor's life is at stake." Consensus: "you shall not stand against the blood of your neighbor" (EMET/LOGOS) — the Hebrew lo ta'amod al dam re'akha is a formal idiom whose meaning is disputed (endangering life by false testimony vs. passive failure to rescue), but the translation should preserve the literal idiom rather than resolve the ambiguity in favor of one interpretation. PNEUMA's paraphrase chooses the passive-rescue reading and loses the blood idiom and its legal connotations.

**V. 17 — "rebuke" vs. "reprove" vs. "confront."** EMET: "you shall surely rebuke." LOGOS: "you shall surely reprove." PNEUMA: "Confront your neighbor directly when he wrongs you." Consensus: "you shall surely rebuke" (EMET) — hokhei'ach tokhiach is the absolute infinitive of yakach (Hiphil), meaning to argue, correct, reprove. "Rebuke" (EMET) and "reprove" (LOGOS) are both legitimate; "rebuke" is more commonly used in English for interpersonal correction (vs. "reprove" which has a slightly more judicial tone). PNEUMA's paraphrase "Confront your neighbor directly when he wrongs you" expands the text by specifying a triggering condition ("when he wrongs you") not present in the Hebrew, and loses the absolute-infinitive emphatic construction entirely.

**V. 17 — "bear sin because of him" vs. "incur sin because of him" vs. "share in his guilt."** EMET: "you shall not bear sin because of him." LOGOS: "lest you incur sin because of him." PNEUMA: "so that you do not share in his guilt." Consensus: "you shall not bear sin because of him" (EMET) — the Hebrew ve-lo tissa alav chet is "and you shall not bear sin upon/because of him." EMET's rendering is the most literal; "bear sin" is the established idiom in this project. LOGOS's "lest you incur" changes the modal structure. PNEUMA's "share in his guilt" is interpretively plausible but loses the Hebrew idiom.

**V. 18 — "bear a grudge" vs. "hold a grudge."** EMET/LOGOS: "bear a grudge." PNEUMA: "hold a grudge." Consensus: "bear a grudge" — the established English idiom "bear a grudge" (EMET/LOGOS) is more traditional and consistent with the Hebrew nasa ("to bear/carry"); "hold" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly more colloquial.

**V. 19 — mixing laws.** EMET: "cause to mate with a different kind [kil'ayim]." LOGOS: "let your cattle breed with a different kind." PNEUMA: "breed two different kinds of animals together." Consensus: "cause to breed with a different kind" — a middle path between EMET's "cause to mate" (slightly clinical) and LOGOS's "let... breed" (passive). For the garment: EMET: "a garment of mixed-stuff [sha'atnez], two kinds." LOGOS: "a garment made of two kinds of material mixed together." PNEUMA: "clothing woven from two different materials." Consensus: "a garment of two kinds of mixed material" — cleaner than EMET's "mixed-stuff [sha'atnez]" (sha'atnez is preserved as a bracketed gloss in the notes, not the text), more compact than LOGOS, and more precise than PNEUMA's "woven" (sha'atnez in Deut 22:11 specifies wool-linen; the weaving method is secondary).

**V. 20 — slave-woman law.** EMET: "slave-woman [shifchah] pledged [necharefet] to a man." LOGOS: "slave, designated for another man." PNEUMA: "slave and pledged to another man." Consensus: "slave-woman pledged to a man" — "slave-woman" is the most precise rendering of shifchah (a female slave; LOGOS's "slave" is gender-neutral where the Hebrew is feminine); "pledged" (EMET/PNEUMA) is preferred over LOGOS's "designated" for necharefet (from the root chrf, suggesting assignment/betrothal). EMET's "redeeming she has not been redeemed" (absolute-infinitive form) is replaced by the smoother "has not been redeemed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); EMET's construction is correct but cumbersome. EMET's "lying with seed" (shikevat zera') is a technical legal phrase; consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA in omitting it from the main clause in favor of clarity: "a man who lies with a woman... a slave-woman pledged to a man." "An inquiry shall be held" (LOGOS) is preferred over EMET's "an inquiry shall be made [biqoret tihyeh]" and PNEUMA's "there must be an inquiry."

**V. 23 — orlah law.** EMET: "treat its fruit as forbidden [va-araltem orlato]... three years it shall be treated as uncircumcised [arelim]." LOGOS: "count its fruit as forbidden... Three years it shall be forbidden to you." PNEUMA: "treat its fruit as forbidden... For three years it is forbidden to you." Consensus: "treat its fruit as forbidden [va-araltem orlato — its foreskin, its uncircumcised fruit]: three years it shall be forbidden to you" — EMET/PNEUMA's "treat as forbidden" is preferred over LOGOS's "count as forbidden." The bracketed gloss in the consensus text preserves the orlah/circumcision metaphor which is theologically significant (the tree's fruit is its "foreskin" in the first three years; the metaphor links agricultural dedication to covenant identity). The gloss is minimal and explanatory rather than a full transliteration.

**V. 24 — hillulim rendering.** EMET: "a praise-offering [hillulim] to the LORD." LOGOS: "an offering of praise to the LORD." PNEUMA: "a holy offering of praise to the LORD." Consensus: "an offering of praise to the LORD" (LOGOS) — "offering of praise" is more natural English than EMET's compound "praise-offering"; PNEUMA adds "holy" which is not in the Hebrew (holiness is expressed by qodesh earlier in the same verse, not by a second adjective on hillulim).

**V. 25 — "add its produce" vs. "increase its yield."** EMET: "for you to add its produce [le-hosif lakhem tevuato]." LOGOS: "to increase its yield for you." PNEUMA: "your harvest will increase." Consensus: "to increase its yield for you" (LOGOS) — the Hiphil of yasaf ("to add, to increase") with tevuato ("its produce/yield") means "to increase [its] yield for you." LOGOS renders this most precisely. EMET's "add its produce" is literal but the English "add" sounds odd with "produce." PNEUMA's "your harvest will increase" loses the reference to the tree's specific yield (tevuato, "its produce").

**V. 26 — blood and divination.** EMET: "eat upon the blood... not practice divination... not practice augury." LOGOS: "eat anything with the blood... not interpret omens or tell fortunes." PNEUMA: "eat anything with the blood still in it... not practice divination or seek omens." Consensus: "eat upon the blood. You shall not practice divination and you shall not practice augury" — "upon the blood" (EMET) is the literal Hebrew al ha-dam; it is the more precise rendering of a disputed phrase (the al may indicate proximity/manner of eating, not merely blood content). LOGOS's "with the blood" and PNEUMA's "with the blood still in it" both interpret "al" as indicating content; EMET's "upon" preserves the ambiguity. For the divinatory practices: EMET's "divination" (nachash) and "augury" (te'onenu) are distinct technical terms; preserving both as distinct renderings is preferable to LOGOS's paraphrases ("interpret omens / tell fortunes") and PNEUMA's merger ("practice divination or seek omens").

**V. 27 — head and beard corners.** EMET: "round off the corner [pe'at] of your head... destroy the corner [pe'at] of your beard." LOGOS: "round off the hair on your temples... mar the edges of your beard." PNEUMA: "cut the hair at the sides of your head... clip the edges of your beard." Consensus: "round off the corner [pe'ah] of your head, and you shall not mar the corner [pe'ah] of your beard" — preserving pe'ah with a single bracketed gloss. "Round off" for naqaf (Hiphil, "to go around, encircle in cutting") is the most precise of the three. "Mar" for lo tashchit ("you shall not destroy") is the established ESV rendering and captures the surprisingly strong verb shachath ("to destroy, ruin") without overstating it as "destroy" (which sounds too extreme for a haircut). PNEUMA's "clip" for tashchit is too mild.

**V. 28 — cutting and tattooing.** EMET: "cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh]... tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa']." LOGOS: "cuts on your body for the dead... tattoo yourselves." PNEUMA: "cut your body for the dead... mark yourself with tattoos." Consensus: "a cutting for the dead [seret la-nefesh] you shall not make in your flesh, and tattoo writing [ketovet qa'aqa'] you shall not put on yourselves" — preserving both technical terms with brackets; they are hapax or near-hapax terms of historical interest. LOGOS's "cuts on your body" and PNEUMA's "cut your body" unnecessarily add "body" (the Hebrew ba-vesarkhem, "in your flesh," is already in the verse). The consensus uses EMET's formulation with "in your flesh" retained from the Hebrew.

**V. 29 — "depravity" vs. "wickedness."** EMET/LOGOS: "depravity." PNEUMA: "wickedness." Consensus: "depravity [zimmah]" — zimmah is a specific Hebrew term (also at Lev 18:17) denoting moral/sexual licentiousness and corruption; "depravity" (EMET/LOGOS) is the more established and precise rendering. "Wickedness" (PNEUMA) is too generic. A bracketed gloss is added since zimmah has appeared in the project before and is a technical term.

**V. 31 — "spirits of the dead" vs. "necromancers" vs. "spiritists."** EMET: "mediums [ha-ovot]... spirits of the dead [yid'onim]." LOGOS: "mediums... necromancers." PNEUMA: "mediums... spiritists." Consensus: "mediums [ha-ovot]... spirits of the dead [yid'onim]" (EMET) — the term yid'onim (from yada, "to know") refers to the familiar spirits or "knowers" that mediums consulted; they are the spirits of the dead, not simply the practitioners. "Necromancers" (LOGOS) conflates the practitioner with the spirit; "spiritists" (PNEUMA) is a modern term with imprecise connotations. EMET's "spirits of the dead" is the most accurate rendering of what yid'onim actually denotes.

**V. 31 — "make yourselves unclean" vs. "to be defiled."** EMET: "to be defiled by them." LOGOS: "make yourselves unclean by them." PNEUMA: "you will be defiled by them." Consensus: "to be defiled by them" (EMET) — the Hebrew le-tum'ah vohem is an infinitive construct: "to become defiled by them." The purpose/result infinitive form ("to be defiled") best captures the Hebrew. LOGOS's "make yourselves unclean" shifts to active voice; PNEUMA's "you will be defiled" shifts to future indicative.

**V. 32 — "gray head" and "elder."** EMET: "Before a gray head [seva]... the face of an elder [zaqen]." LOGOS: "You shall stand up before the gray head... honor the face of an old man." PNEUMA: "Stand up in the presence of the aged... Show respect for the elderly." Consensus: "Before a gray head you shall rise, and you shall honor the face of an elder" — EMET's "gray head" (seva) is the literal Hebrew and evocative image; LOGOS confirms it. PNEUMA's "the aged" and "the elderly" are interpretive generalizations that lose the concrete image. "Elder" (EMET) for zaqen is preferred over LOGOS's "old man" (which loses the social-role dimension of zaqen as an elder/authority figure) and PNEUMA's "the elderly" (too generic). "Rise" for taqum is more literal than LOGOS's "stand up" and is a natural English word for the respectful gesture.

**V. 33 — "sojourner" and "wrong/oppress."** EMET: "sojourner [ger]... you shall not oppress [lo tonu] him." LOGOS: "sojourner... you shall not wrong him." PNEUMA: "foreigner...stranger... do not mistreat them." Consensus: "sojourner... you shall not wrong him" — "sojourner" is the binding project ruling for ger; PNEUMA's "foreigner" and "stranger" are rejected. For lo tonu (from yanah, "to oppress, mistreat, harm"): both "oppress" (EMET) and "wrong" (LOGOS) are legitimate; "wrong" (LOGOS) is slightly broader and avoids the possible connotation of economic oppression specifically, matching the general prohibition more accurately. PNEUMA's "mistreat them" uses plural "them" inconsistently with the singular ger, though the law applies collectively.

**V. 34 — "sojourner" ruling and love command.** EMET/LOGOS: "sojourner." PNEUMA: "foreigner" throughout. Consensus: "sojourner" — binding ruling. PNEUMA's substitution of "foreigner" for ger at vv. 33–34 is the most consequential terminology violation in this chapter and is rejected in full. "As a native among you" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "native-born Israelite" — ezrach is "native" or "indigenous person," and while typically this means a native Israelite in context, spelling it out as "native-born Israelite" adds a specification not in the Hebrew. "For sojourners you were in the land of Egypt" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "you were foreigners in Egypt" for the same reason: gerim is the Hebrew word used, and the echo of ger/gerim is the theological point of the verse — Israel was the very thing (ger) it is now commanded to protect.

**V. 35 — measures.** EMET: "in measure [ba-midah], in weight [ba-mishqal], or in liquid measure [ba-meshurah]." LOGOS: "in measures of length or weight or quantity." PNEUMA: "in measuring length, weight, or quantity." Consensus: "in measure, in weight, or in quantity" — the three Hebrew terms midah (measure/dimension), mishqal (weight), and meshurah (liquid measure/quantity) cover all commercial transactions. "Measure, weight, or quantity" is the most natural tricolon in English and preserves the three distinct categories. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "length" for midah is an over-specification; midah covers any dimension of measure, not only length.

**V. 36 — "balances/weights of righteousness" vs. "just balances/weights."** EMET: "Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq], stones of righteousness [avne tsedeq], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq]." LOGOS: "just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin." PNEUMA: "honest scales, honest weights, and honest measures — dry and liquid alike." Consensus: "Balances of righteousness [mozne tsedeq], weights of righteousness [avne tsedeq], an ephah of righteousness [eifat tsedeq], and a hin of righteousness [hin tsedeq]" — the fourfold repetition of tsedeq ("righteousness") is the theological point: the same word used for judicial integrity (v. 15) and for the prohibition on injustice (v. 35) is applied to measuring instruments. LOGOS's "just" and PNEUMA's "honest" both flatten this deliberate verbal link. The bracketed Hebrew terms are retained in the consensus as they are technical terms; "weights" is preferred over EMET's "stones" (avne tsedeq) since the English "weights" is clearer than "stones" (even though the Hebrew avne, "stones," refers to stone counterweights on a balance scale — this is preserved in the notes).

**V. 36 — "brought you out from" vs. "brought you out of."** EMET: "who brought you out from the land of Egypt." LOGOS: "who brought you out of the land of Egypt." PNEUMA: "who brought you out of Egypt." Consensus: "who brought you out of the land of Egypt" — "of" (LOGOS) is the standard English preposition for this formula; "from" (EMET) is defensible but "of the land" is the established phrase. PNEUMA's omission of "the land of" truncates the formula that consistently reads "eretz mitsrayim" ("the land of Egypt").

**V. 37 — "judgments/rules/ordinances."** EMET: "my statutes and all my judgments." LOGOS: "my statutes and all my rules." PNEUMA: "my statutes and all my ordinances." Consensus: "my statutes and all my ordinances" — mishpatai is the second term; "ordinances" (PNEUMA) is the established project rendering for mishpatim (cf. Lev 18:4–5 consensus), more encompassing than "rules" (LOGOS) and more current than "judgments" (EMET, which has a judicial-verdict connotation in modern English).

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the love commands (vv. 18, 34):** The two climactic verses of the chapter — ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamokha (v. 18) and ve-ahavta lo kamokha (v. 34) — are reproduced with Hebrew transliterations in the consensus text body because they are among the most quoted phrases in all of scripture and their precise wording carries interpretive weight. The LXX rendering of both (ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν, v. 18; ἀγαπήσεις αὐτὸν ὡς σεαυτόν, v. 34) is the form that enters the New Testament through Jesus (Matt 22:39; Mark 12:31), Paul (Rom 13:9; Gal 5:14), and James (Jas 2:8) without alteration. The LXX chain of transmission from these two verses directly to the NT is one of the most significant in the entire Bible.

**On ger = sojourner (vv. 10, 33–34):** The binding ruling that ger = "sojourner" throughout is grounded in the legal-social specificity of the term. The ger is not any foreigner but a resident alien with ongoing ties to the Israelite community — someone who has left their native land and taken up permanent or semi-permanent residence among Israelites, without full native legal standing. "Foreigner" (PNEUMA's consistent choice) suggests a visitor or outsider; "sojourner" preserves the meaning of dwelling-among. The echo at v. 34 — "for gerim were you in the land of Egypt" — uses the same noun applied to Israel's own historical condition, making the word-choice theologically critical: Israel was not merely "in" Egypt; it was ger there, an alien with all the vulnerability that entails.

**On tsedeq across the chapter (vv. 15, 35–36):** The same word tsedeq — righteousness — governs judicial integrity (v. 15: "in righteousness you shall judge"), the prohibition on commercial fraud (v. 35: "no injustice in judgment"), and the specific commercial instruments (v. 36: balances, weights, ephah, hin "of righteousness"). This cross-domain application of tsedeq is one of the chapter's most distinctive theological moves: there is no separation between legal justice and commercial honesty; both are expressions of the same divine attribute.

**On pe'ah and leqet (vv. 9–10):** These terms are introduced with bracketed glosses at v. 9 (first occurrence); they are not repeated in brackets at v. 10 since they do not reappear in v. 10 (v. 10 uses different terms: lo te'olel for stripping the vineyard, and peret for fallen grapes). The book of Ruth is the narrative embodiment of this law (Ruth 2:2–23), and the chapter's pairing of "the poor and the sojourner" as its beneficiaries (v. 10) directly introduces Ruth the Moabitess as the paradigmatic ger who benefits from Israelite gleaning law.

**On the orlah law (vv. 23–25):** The circumcision metaphor applied to fruit trees — the fruit is the tree's "foreskin" (orlah) for three years — links agricultural dedication to covenant-body theology. The bracketed gloss in the consensus text ("its foreskin, its uncircumcised fruit") preserves this deliberately for the reader without forcing a choice between the literal metaphor and the practical ruling.

**On seret la-nefesh and ketovet qa'aqa' (v. 28):** Both are preserved as bracketed glosses because they are rare/unique Hebrew terms of antiquarian interest. Seret la-nefesh (cutting for the dead) is a self-laceration mourning practice attested in the ancient Near East and prohibited also at Deut 14:1. Ketovet qa'aqa' (tattoo writing) contains the only occurrence of qa'aqa' in the Hebrew Bible; the term clearly denotes permanent skin-marking.

---
